flashcards.back: The branch of computer science dealing with creation, manipulation, and representation of visual information using computers. It converts data into visual forms for effective communication.
The number of times per second the display screen is redrawn, measured in Hertz (Hz). Higher refresh rates produce smoother images (e.g., 60Hz = 60 redraws/second).
A portion of memory that holds the complete image to be displayed on screen. Each pixel's color value is stored in the frame buffer for display.
A display system where the electron beam scans from top to bottom, left to right, illuminating pixels row by row. Uses a frame buffer to store pixel data.
A display system where the electron beam moves directly to draw lines and curves between specified endpoints. Lines are drawn one at a time.
The total number of pixels that can be displayed on screen, expressed as (horizontal × vertical). Higher resolution means more pixels and sharper images.
The ratio of width to height of the display screen. Standard aspect ratios include 4:3 (standard), 16:9 (widescreen), and 16:10.

flashcards.front: What is an Input Device in Computer Graphics?
flashcards.back: Devices used to interact with graphics systems: Mouse, Keyboard, Joystick, Light Pen, Digitizer, Touch Panel, Trackball.

flashcards.front: What is an Output Device in Computer Graphics?
flashcards.back: Devices that display or produce visual output: CRT Monitor, LCD/LED Display, Printer (Dot Matrix, Laser, Inkjet), Plotter.
The sequence of steps to convert 3D scene description to 2D image: Modeling → Geometry Processing → Rasterization → Display.
The smallest addressable unit on a display screen. Each pixel has a specific color and location. Short for 'picture element'.

flashcards.front: What is an Active Matrix Display?
flashcards.back: A type of LCD using thin-film transistors (TFT) for each pixel, providing better image quality, faster response, and wider viewing angles.
The number of bits used to represent each pixel's color. Also called bit depth. Common values: 8-bit (256 colors), 24-bit (16.7 million colors).
A technique using two frame buffers - one for display and one for drawing. When drawing completes, buffers are swapped to eliminate flicker.
A display technique where odd and even scan lines are refreshed alternately. Reduces bandwidth but can cause flicker.
